item.title: Burger bombs
item.description: Garlic Parmesan Cheeseburger Bombs Ingredients: 1 lb ground beef 1 cup shredded cheddar cheese 1/2 cup cream cheese, softened 1 tsp garlic powder 1 tsp onion powder Salt and pepper, to taste 1 can (16 oz) refrigerated biscuit dough 1/4 cup grated Parmesan cheese 2 tbsp butter, melted Fresh parsley, chopped (for garnish) Instructions: Preheat your oven to 375°F (190°C) and line a baking sheet with parchment paper. In a skillet over medium heat, brown the ground beef until fully cooked. Drain any excess grease. Add garlic powder, onion powder, salt, and pepper. Mix in the cream cheese and shredded cheddar cheese until well combined, then remove from heat. Open the can of biscuit dough and separate it into individual biscuits. Flatten each biscuit into a circle. Place a spoonful of the beef and cheese mixture in the center of each flattened biscuit. Fold the dough over the filling and pinch the edges to seal completely. In a small bowl, mix melted butter and grated Parmesan cheese. Brush the tops of the cheeseburger bombs with the garlic Parmesan mixture. Arrange the bombs on the prepared baking sheet and bake for 12-15 minutes, or until golden brown. Let the bombs cool slightly before serving. Garnish with fresh parsley for a pop of color! Enjoy these delicious, cheesy bites with a touch of garlic and Parmesan!

item.title: It's time for yet another unhinged XL Sunday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends
what kind of hornet/wasp (?) is that?
mmm hmm
reasons
they say i never do one for the ladies
i love the castle and pirate ones from my era. but havent they re-released some?
i will defend bic to the death, 1 of their lighters lasts me months and i smoke a good bit of weed
how to fold ingredients
accurate
wait what
i would die for cheezit
nope
no thank you
gross
he finally found inner peas
i would've wanted that as a kid just for the gun
yeah. all of those
that's my time.. have a great night gang!!

item.title: Transition Timeline 10 yrs/ thriving
item.description:
10 years ago I was ready to leave this life, having been born with a brain that did not match my body… I decided to give myself one last chance and seek help.
This is me today. No filters, just ten years of MTF HRT and the drive to live my best life. This is the first time in my life I have felt happy, and whole, and like myself.

item.title: Scientists find a 35,000-year-old saber-toothed kitten in the Siberian permafrost
item.description: "An "almost perfectly preserved" saber-toothed cub has been unearthed in Russia's Sakha Republic, 35,000 years after it died. The cat is of the species Homotherium latidens, a distant relative of the more familiar Smilodon." CLONE THE KITTY! https://www.npr.org/2024/11/16/nx-s1-5193845/35000-year-old-kitten-siberia-frozen

item.title: The original zero suit Samus
item.description:
For those too young to remember the original Metroid games, the green haired, pink/magenta leotard outfit was from the first Metroid game. If you entered your name as "Justin Bailey" it would let you play the game in that outfit. The name itself is kind of a secondary Easter Egg, since the word "bailey" is another word for swimsuit. So the name basically means "just in swimsuit". Cosplay by Phavorianne https://www.acparadise.com/acp/display.php?a=72
The black two piece outfit was originally a reward for beating the second Metroid game within a certain amount of time. At the end of the game you would get a screen showing Samus posing. If you beat the game within a certain time it would show her take off her helmet, and if you got an even better time it would show her without her armor, wearing this casual outfit of hers. (The outfit made appearances in later games with changes to the color and style.)

item.dc:creator: Necridos

item.title: The Big Chonk Restoration has begun. Dark gods help me.
item.description:
It's happening! I'm pretty sure this machine is going to kick my ass but it's happening! After a lot of procrastinating and doubting, I'm finally kicking off the brakes and getting started on this absolute unit of a machine. This was one of those "eyes bigger than my stomach" impulse buys, only in my case I didn't take into account how stupidly HUGE this thing would be. Built in 1912, this Model 465 National Cash Register weighs... too much. I can't lift it. I knew I'd fucked up when I arrived to the seller's house and she had her jacked son waiting to help carry it up from their basement and into my car. Even after getting it onto my passenger seat (and smashing the padding flat), it was too wide for me to buckle it in. So I had to drive home with one arm holding it back - barely - from tumbling into the footwell every time I braked. Super fun when you're on I-95. I don't regret it though. I've -never- gotten a shot at buying one of these mahogany box registers. They're always priced around a thousand bucks or more and there's no way in hell I'll ever spend that much into something that isn't already a finished restoration. This one popped up on Marketplace for much less, and the seller was willing to come down to $550 which is close to the top of my budget range. Apparently they'd gotten several offers from my fellow collectors, but all of them were out of state and were trying to get them to freight this thing over to them. Pays to live locally! :D
This thing is wide. Wider than the last monster I restored, and that one I could only barely lift. To safely move this thing around my house, I had to buy a hydraulic table cart with a 500lb capacity. Fun fact, Vevor originally screwed up and sent me a busted cart which I had to mail back to them. They then sent me *two* replacements instead of one, and I ain't telling them diddly. Not sure what I'll do with the extra. Anyway. The reason this register has had me spooked is due to its bulk. If I start this project, that means I have to start dealing with it. Horse help me, but yesterday I finally buckled up and got started.
Let's get the biggest question out of the way: Does it work? It does! Barely. It actually *hates* working. I can feel its rusty, resistant hatred with ever degree I turn the crank. It is not a happy machine, as you can hear from the shriek of metal scraping against metal. The reason I can't hold this camera still is because I'm having to put my weight into finishing that crank turn. If I don't, there's a risk it can jam, so being gentle is kind of out the window.
The next obvious thing is that the marble shelf is missing completely. At first, I didn't know it was meant to have one! I thought the fancy mahogany (and yes, that's mahogany) drawer box was supposed to serve as a shelf. Then I noticed the conspicuous rectangular void at the very bottom of the register body, and the vacant screw holes. So... there's a $100 purchase in my very near future. @.@
If I learned anything from the last crank machine I fixed up, starting with the printer box makes disassembling the case much easier. So that's where we'll begin. Right away, I can see that the scrollwork cage is dented. I might be able to hammer that out, but that's for future Ellie to worry about.
One of the reasons I really wanted to get this machine was for this little mystery. I've never seen this accessory before, not even on listings I've passed on. Some experimenting made it clear that whatever it is, it adds additional information to the receipt when it prints. I'm just not sure yet what it's meant to add that the register can't already do on its own. More research is needed!
It's pretty loosey goosey on the printer box, which was part of the reason the seller was willing to negotiate their price a bit lower. My concern was that a bolt had sheared off somewhere, but that would turn out not to be the case.
The printer box doesn't actually have any bolts or hinges that keep it in place. Just a simple latch and the whole thing slides loose as a unit. Here you can see traces of the forbidden "green death." Basically, moisture has penetrated the nickel plating and started oxidizing the bronze layer underneath. When this gets cleaned away, the exposed bronze will usually turn red once everything is finished. It's not a very pretty look, but I prefer retaining the age spots to spending thousands (not an exaggeration) of dollars having it completely replated and looking artificially new.
Just like the last crank register, this one has a significant dent on the printer box. I groaned when I saw this. Last time, knocking this dent out was a pain. You can only pound on welded bronze so much before the weld cracks, and I left some of the dent in place with my last restoration to avoid that.
And here's the reason why the accessory... thing... was so loose. Literally just a loose bolt. Pretty sure I can fix that. :D
I'm not encouraged by the visual state of the printer mechanism, but in all honesty... I've never gotten one of these printers working again. Not for a lack of confidence or drive, but because finding the correct ink and paper is next to impossible unless you somehow roll a Nat 20 on eBay. There's plenty of NCR receipt paper out there to be purchased, but NCR was absolutely lousy about choosing a standard width and sticking to it. Still, I've found a video from another restorer explaining how to break down and clean this exact printer. So maybe I'll take a shot at it anyway, just so it looks nice.
Ahem, what have we here? If I didn't know any better, I'd say that's a custom receipt embossing spool!
IT IS A CUSTOM SPOOL.
At least, it was. I *might* be able to save this, but I'm going to be realistic and not get my hopes up. The ink residue looks like it served as a wick for moisture, and there's a lot of corrosion. This will be its own project.
Hello, nurse! Another custom spool, this one apparently being intended for printing on the back side of the receipt. I only know that because of the restoration video I happened across, and my knowledge kind of fizzles after that. Still, something new! Also, notice how there are counter wheels built into the spool itself? Half of those, the ones on the far right, are operated by the lever accessory from earlier. The mystery continues, hooohooo!
Alright, enough dickering around. Time to disassemble this monster and get a sense of what kind of nightmare I'm getting myself into. First up: I need to get that accessory thing off. I can't do that without first freeing up the lever guides.
Off she comes, and of course the housing for the box doesn't want to come off. I had to set the camera down and do some creative wiggling before I figured out there was yet another loose bolt limiting my ability to maneuver it around.
Hey kid, you want some rust? I got the good stuff. Admittedly, this is one of the reasons I prefer brass registers over nickel registers. When the brass gets damaged and oxidation occurs, it's pretty hard to notice once its polished and clean. When the nickel gets damaged and the base iron or bronze start to oxidize, there's a big splotchy contrast in metals. I've looked at what it would take to build my own plating setup, and it's just not something I want to do. So when I choose a register to restore, I take into account the condition of the plating. Having restored a couple of really badly neglected machines now, I sort of want to avoid the rust buckets where I can. BUT! I have this one now, and I'm gonna keep working.
These are the numbers that the lever assembly tinkers with. Now I'm thinking the "cylinder" I spotted might actually just be a rotating plate that glides above these arced numbers.
Anyhoo, onto the larger disassembly! Did you notice how long this post is? If you haven't yet, I'm so sorry. xD First off, I gotta take apart this decorative little guy.
An example of the photos I filtered out of this post. These are the snaps I take for my own documentation. It'll be a month or so before I start reassembling, and by then I'll have probably forgotten which fastener goes to which part. There's so much variety in this machine, I need to take pictures so I don't mix them up.
The front bit's off, now for the side bit.
And here you can see the different type of bolt they used. Thankfully, NCR always uses the same threading, but the length, style, and diameter change way too much to trust to guesswork. I ain't stopping halfway through a rebuild just to realize I screwed up on step two.
Another reminder picture for myself. This pawl arm needs to come off before I can remove the side panels. Also, hooray for not having enough clearance to remove the bolt! There was some creative use of English during this step, but I eventually got it free.
Before I take panels off, I need to start removing the little doodads attached to it, including the NCR nameplate. I've learned the hard way not to polish this, because I'll be left with a blank plate. ;)
After that, the lockable counter lids need to come off. Each of these uses the same key and were usually only available to the store owner. They serve to stop cashiers from being able to see or manipulate the tallies for each type of transaction, minimizing the temptation of theft. Prior to National Cash Register, cash boxes were a simple matter of a lock and key. There were other brands of mechanical cash registers in the late 1800's when NCR arose, but the security measures NCR invented would become the gold standard that eventually allowed them to dominate the point of sale market for over a century.
I'm missing the slotted plate that screws onto the lock cover on the far left, which stinks. Normally a slip of paper with employee names would be put there, indicating which employee was assigned to which teller key (A, B, D, E, H, K). One of those keys has to be pressed in order for the machine to cycle, otherwise it'll stay locked out.
With the front panel clear, I moved onto the crank handle. Right away, I noticed it's not hanging straight down in the proper neutral position. That means someone took it out and put it back in crooked. Not a real issue, but evidence that someone was tinkering with it at some point.
Not... ideal. This is the original bolt, but someone was apparently having a rough day when they used it last. I'll have to see if I can't grind this smooth and cut out the driver slot a little cleaner later on. Suffice to say, having the slot rounded out made taking the handle off a pain in the ass.
No broken teeth, thankfully.
You sit there and think about what you did.
Next step is to take out all the bolts holding the face plate down. This panel comes off first because I said so.
But not before removing this reset tab. This little guy pops out the keys you pressed if you made a mistake. Also, it's missing one of its screws and that kind of bugs me. Like... just keep your screws in one spot if you're going to take things apart! Augh!
I'm gonna find you a buddy. -_-
Please ignore that the bottom key in each column is already mysteriously depressed and tucked behind the panel. I totally didn't have to fiddle with this thing for five minutes until I found out the proper way to free it from the machine. It totally came off this smoothly the first try.
Making progress! So far I'm not seeing any corrosion or excess of gunk in the guts, so that tells me all that stickiness in the handle has something to do with the gearing mechanism and the printer mechanism. Everything inside, at least, is clean.
Off with her head! Next step was to pop the indicator wheel window off.
And to break it down for cleaning. And of course, one of the glass panes was busted. Add that to the list of things to replace...
Obligatory top down view. Nice.
Now to take off this upper face plate. Notice how there are screws here...
...and not here. HOW HARD IS IT TO KEEP TRACK OF SCREWS. Adding *fasteners* to the shopping list, because I don't have spares in nickel.
Stupid stinky faceplate and its stupid missing screws. Who only screws down one side? Screw half of each side! Blargh!
The FILTH behind it. Fuck me this is gross. Dirt, grease, oxidation, the whole menu of yuck.
At least the indicator wheels are mostly clean. Also, once again, nice.
Know what's not fun? Pivoting two hundred pounds of metal to see the back. :D Ah well, gripe-gripe-gripe. Back panel looks pretty good! Dirty, sure, but very little oxidation. Benefit of being shoved up against a wall for a few decades. Missing a screw, though. I see that hole. I am good at finding holes.
...huh. I don't know why the nickel finish is worn through on the back, but it is. Manufacturing defect, maybe? I dunno, but there it is!
Ah. I see now why someone was tinkering. I guess I can't fault them. Cheaper to slap on a few layers of electrical tape that nobody will see than it is to buy new glass. GUESS I'LL BUY THE GLASS THEN. xD *adds second pane to shopping list*
Look at that bare backside.
Alright, onto the side panels. Zip-zap-zoop, off come the screws.
Take it all in, steampunk nerds. You wish your cogs were as big as mine. :D But in all seriousness, this is likely to be a pain spot on this machine. If the resistance isn't in here, it's on the other side, but I'm going to take a wild guess and assume Joe Schmoe was in here spraying WD-40. These gears look too clean.
Inside of the same panel, and I see once again somebody was slacking in the plating department. Tsk tsk. ;) At the very top is the back of transaction counter. If you scroll back up to when the panel was installed, you can see four black dots. Those are the windows for the counter and normally you can see numbers through them.
Of course, you can't if the window is caked in several generations of grime. That "dust" had the consistency of play-doh. Mm-mm-good.
Someone was in here cleaning and that bothers me. Looks like I'm going to need to buy machine grease and spend a lot of time cycling this thing until whatever's grinding gets lubed up.
A few hours into the disassembly and we find ourselves back where we started. If you remember the last crank machine I took apart, this isn't one panel but two! Time to break them apart.
So many colors. Blech.
Definitely some potential for problems in here. It doesn't look like the last guy bothered to open this side up, so I'm seeing a ton of old grease which is good. But I'm also seeing bits of paper hanging around as well. I don't think this machine would give a crap if some paper got mixed into the grease, but I could be wrong. Something to pay attention to when I start investigating deeper.
I'm not sure what I'll do with these keys. I've replaced the caps on other machines, but not this kind. These don't have glass protection over them either. Just really old celluloid. Ideally, I'd like to fix these up. I'm just not sure where I'll find the resources just yet.
As for the covers on those counters, they have to come off. Same with the strips that screw down around both sides of each key column. The screws that hold them down are super small, have very few threads, and like to squirt out of my fingers thanks to the grease that is getting everywhere by now. Also, there are a lot of them! Super fun! :D
After half an hour of dropping screws, tracking them down, and managing not to strip the heads off any of them (thank horse for steel screws), they're all off. And oh boy is it rusty underneath. I won't be able to scrub all this off or soak it in solution, so what I'll end up doing is try to clean up as much as I can without introducing grit into the key channels. They're already sticky enough as it is, and I don't want to make it worse.
And lastly, the metal strip that hides the running total from the cashier.
Looks a lot easier when its' done!
Cover your eyes! It's nekkid!
( ͡° ͜ʖ ͡°)
Moving onto the drawer box, the cash drawer is definitely... well used. I've always been leery about taking these apart. Woodworking is decidedly not a skillset I have, and I don't want to break any of these dividers by trying to pry them out. I might try sanding them, I might leave them as-is and just wash them out. Normally I wouldn't consider it at all if it weren't for the extent of wear and tear. I mean, one of the dividers is held on with duct tape. Ugh. Oh hey, spare screws!
The inside of the box is a little dirty, but looks great. No repairs needed here!
Now to remove the mechanism from the drawer box. This is as easy as removing four lag bolts.
Just bend back the tab on this retention thingy (I don't know the right name for it, so it's a retention thingy).
And after a lot of cursing, remove the nut and give the bolt a gentle tap with a hammer until it falls into the box.
If NCR was serious about one thing, it was about not fucking around with the quality of their parts. I'm pretty sure the lug nuts on my car could thread onto this thing. And there's four of em.
Despite having so much extra weight pulled off of it, lifting the mechanism off the drawer box was not easy. I managed to do it without hurting myself, though, but it still weighs as much as a bag of concrete.
As for the drawer box, I don't think I'll need to do much more to it than wash it in wood oil and set it aside. Booyeah!
The trouble with these mechanisms is that there are two levers that hang down from the bottom which interact with the drawer catch. This makes it impossible to set it down in the middle of, say, a workbench without snapping them off. So the ass end of the mechanism needs to hang over the side of my workbench until I find a couple of 2x4s to sit it on. Don't worry, it won't fall off.
And this is where I'm at now! You're all caught up! Once I get this seated on some boards I'll put the crank back on and start looking into what's causing it to stick during operation. While I work on that, I'll start ordering replacement glass, a marble shelf, and all the other stuff that's busted or missing on this machine. This definitely won't be a two or three weekend project. Not if I don't want to half-ass it. It's probably going to cost enough to make me wish I'd haggled harder, but that's always the risk when buying something that spent more years than I've been alive moldering away in someone's storage unit. The goal is to revitalize these sturdy bastards, not scrub off some dirt and leave the hard stuff for the next person. Gotta tough it out. :) What I'm saying is that it might be a week or two until the next update, so hang in there! If you've ever found yourself looking at these restorations and wishing you could help out, my Ko-Fi is open for expressly that purpose! A spare ten bucks can pay for an entire bottle of degreaser and twenty will cover gloves! I don't expect Imgur to fund these projects top to bottom - that's my bill to pay for getting into teaching myself this skill - but I'll never say no to anyone who wants to help ease the cost of all the little things I buy to do the actual work. If that's something you'd like to contribute to, even just a few bucks, I'd be very grateful. https://ko-fi.com/elbowdeepinahorse/ Thanks for making it all the way to the bottom, too! This was a long one! :D
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse

item.title: Returning stolen goods pt 107
item.description: I have always been like this, having to be extremely specific about these little details that absolutely do not matter. So cause of this scene, my mom and I just say "15 ½" whenever we notice we are stuck in a loop.
item.dc:creator: MrsPrendsen
